does: This paper identifies and systematizes a new class of security vulnerabilities in brain-computer interface (BCI) systems connected to large language model agents. The authors demonstrate that signal-level perturbations, context injections, and adaptive attacks can manipulate routed actions while remaining undetected by standard EEG or text-based monitoring. They formalize route safety through a Route-Safety Audit Contract specifying minimal logging schemas, hierarchical denominators, and endpoint specifications. The work proves an audit-schema separation theorem showing that decoder agreement and marginal robustness alone cannot identify joint terms controlling action routing. The authors instantiate their framework on EEG motor imagery data (EEGMMI dataset, 5,400 events) using split-conformal calibration on an EEG confirmation channel to characterize false-accept rates under different threat archetypes.
connects: This work extends security auditing frameworks from machine learning systems to the emerging domain of neural interface agents. It builds on prior research in adversarial robustness of neural decoders, agent safety monitoring, and conformal prediction methods. The paper responds to increasing deployment of BCI systems with language model backends by treating the interface between neural decoding and tool-use authorization as a distinct attack surface requiring dedicated analysis. It leverages conformal calibration techniques from distribution-free uncertainty quantification and combines them with threat modeling approaches from secure system design, adapting these established methods to the novel context of brain-signal authentication.
enables: This work opens investigation into BCI-LLM agent security as a systematic research area. It enables development of cryptographically-grounded authentication schemes for neural interfaces, exploration of hardware-assisted confirmation channels for brain signals, and design of robust monitoring systems that can detect signal injection attacks. The audit contract framework provides a foundation for standardizing safety requirements and logging practices across BCI-agent systems. Future work can extend conformal methods to multi-modal confirmation, investigate adaptive attacks against proposed defenses, and develop threat models for closed-loop neural interfaces. The decomposition theorems may also guide design of inherently safer decoder architectures and routing protocols that minimize the attack surface between neural signal interpretation and agent action authorization.
